title: Dynamical Tsallis WIMP Freeze-Out and Residual Memory Channels in the Radiation Sector
abstract: In this work we generalize the thermal decoupling, or freeze-out, of weakly interacting massive particle dark matter within the Tsallis nonextensive formalism. The generalization is implemented through $q$-deformed distribution functions obtained from the maximum entropy principle with Curado-Tsallis constraints. The Tsallis parameter $q$, which measures deviations from extensivity with respect to the limit $q=1$, is promoted to a dynamical quantity depending on the dimensionless variable $x=m_χ/T$, where $m_χ$ is the dark matter mass. This dynamical evolution is characterized by a relaxation toward extensivity, while requiring that the nonextensive deformation is not completely erased before freeze-out. We solve the Boltzmann equation assuming a sectorial deformation, where only the dark matter equilibrium abundance is generalized and the radiation background remains extensive. The relic abundance is computed for different dark matter masses and initial values of the Tsallis parameter. From this evolution, we extract the residual value $q_χ^{\rm fo}$ at freeze-out, which is then used as the initial input for a phenomenological memory channel. This channel propagates the residual nonextensivity into the radiation sector, specifically into the electron-positron plasma and neutrinos, while photons are kept extensive in order to avoid direct tensions with CMB physics. The resulting deformation modifies the neutrino energy density and the photon reheating contribution, producing a correction to $N_{\rm eff}$. We compare the predicted values with the compressed CMB+BAO constraint on $N_{\rm eff}$ and find that the residual-memory scenario can remain phenomenologically compatible with current bounds.
summary: This work explores how nonextensive statistical mechanics, quantified by the Tsallis parameter $q$, can modify the standard calculation of WIMP dark matter freeze-out. The resulting residual nonextensivity is then modeled as a 'memory' imprinted onto the radiation bath, leading to a calculable correction to $N_{	ext{eff}}$ that remains consistent with current cosmological observations.
main_problem: The paper generalizes WIMP dark matter freeze-out using Tsallis nonextensive statistics and models how the resulting residual nonextensivity is transferred into the early universe's radiation sector.
main_result: The residual-memory scenario can remain phenomenologically compatible with current CMB+BAO constraints on the effective number of relativistic species ($N_{	ext{eff}}$).
method: The authors solve the Boltzmann equation using q-deformed distribution functions and employ a 'memory channel' ansatz to couple the dark matter freeze-out residual to the neutrino and electron-positron plasma.
model_or_system: The system is the early universe plasma containing WIMPs ($\chi$), photons, $e^\pm$, and neutrinos. The nonextensivity is modeled via the Tsallis parameter $q$, which dynamically evolves during freeze-out.
key_observables: Effective number of relativistic species ($N_{	ext{eff}}$), relic abundance ($\Omega_{\chi}h^2$), and the residual Tsallis parameter ($q_{f\chi}$).
important_parameters:
Tsallis parameter ($q$), dark matter mass ($m_\chi$), and memory transfer efficiency parameters ($\lambda_{e\pm}, \lambda_
u$).
main_assumptions: The dark matter sector is generalized using Tsallis statistics, while the photon background is kept extensive ($q_\gamma=1$). The memory transfer is treated phenomenologically via an inheritance ansatz.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of the Tsallis parameter $q_\chi$ during freeze-out and show the dependence of the required annihilation cross-section on initial $q_\chi$ to match the observed relic density.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by first generalizing the WIMP freeze-out using dynamical Tsallis statistics, determining the residual nonextensivity ($q_{f\chi}$). It then introduces a memory channel to propagate this residual nonextensivity into the radiation sector, calculating the resulting modification to $N_{	ext{eff}}$ and comparing it to cosmological bounds.
why_interesting: The application of nonextensive statistical mechanics (Tsallis formalism) to fundamental cosmological processes like dark matter freeze-out provides a non-standard extension to standard thermal field theory calculations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Evolution of qχ as a function of the freeze-out vari- able x = mχ/T. The trajectories are obtained from Eq. (7) for different initial values of the Tsallis parameter in the dark sector.

Fig. 2: Dynamic-q freeze-out in the dark sector for different dark matter masses mχ. Solid curves show Yχ(x), dashed curves show Yχ,eq,q(x), and markers indicate the freeze-out points xfo. The color scale denotes the initial Tsallis parameter qχ,i.

Fig. 3: Relic Density obtained from the dynamic-q freeze-out evolution shown in Fig. 2. The panels correspond to the same benchmark dark matter masses mχ and initial values of the dark sector Tsallis parameter qχ,i. The horizontal dashed line indicates the reference value Ωχh2 ≃0.120.

Fig. 4: Relic density target curves in the (mχ, σ0) plane. For each initial value of the dark sector Tsallis parameter qχ,i, the required annihilation strength σreq 0 is obtained by imposing Ωχh2 ≃0.120 after solving the dynamic-q freeze-out equation. The horizontal dashed line denotes the reference value of σ0 used in the benchmark relic den- sity evolution.

Fig. 5: Projection of the relic density target surface onto the (mχ, qχ,i) plane. The color scale gives log10 σreq 0 , where σreq 0 is the annihilation strength required to sat- isfy Ωχh2 ≃0.120. The contour lines show the logarithmic shift with respect to the extensive target curve, summariz- ing the degeneracy between the WIMP mass, the initial nonextensive deformation, and the effective annihilation strength.
